summary: This lesson teaches the Spanish accent/stress rules: words ending in a vowel, n, or s have natural stress on the penultimate syllable; words ending in other consonants have stress on the final syllable. Written accents indicate a break from these rules. It also covers accent marks used to differentiate monosyllables and question words.

teachings.conceptName: Spanish stress and written accent rules
teachings.summary: Spanish has two natural stress rules: (1) words ending in a vowel, n, or s have stress on the penultimate (second-to-last) syllable; (2) words ending in any other consonant have stress on the final syllable. These endings (vowel, n, s) correspond to what verb forms end in. A written accent (tilde) appears only when a word breaks these natural rules.
teachings.rule: Ends in vowel/n/s -> stress penultimate syllable (no accent needed). Ends in other consonant -> stress final syllable (no accent needed). Written accent = the word breaks the rule above.

examples.english: End in vowel/n/s, stress on penultimate -- no written accent
End in -r (not vowel/n/s), stress on final syllable -- no written accent
End in -n but stress is on final syllable -- breaks rule, so written accent on ó
End in vowel but stress on final syllable -- preterite 3rd person always has accent
End in vowel, stress on end -- written accent
Lemon -- ends in n but stress on final syllable, so accent on ó
Health -- ends in d, stress on end, follows rule, no accent

teachings.notes: The rule is easy to remember by looking at present tense verbs: they end in vowels, n's, or s's, and always stress the penultimate syllable (with the one exception of estar: estás, están, está which have written accents).

teachings.summary: Some one-syllable words that are spelled the same are differentiated by a written accent. The accent does not change pronunciation -- it only distinguishes meaning in writing.
teachings.rule: Monosyllable pairs: sí (yes) vs si (if); mí (me, after prepositions) vs mi (my); tú (you) vs tu (your); sé (I know) vs se (reflexive/phonetic pronoun).

examples.english: Yes I want it. (sí with accent = yes)
If I want it. (si without accent = if)
I give it to him. (se without accent)

teachings.summary: Question words take a written accent when used as questions, but not when used as non-question conjunctions/relative words.
teachings.rule: dónde (where?) vs donde (where, relative); cómo (how?) vs como (like/as)

examples.spanish: Voy a verlo donde vimos el fútbol.
examples.english: I'm going to see him where we saw the football. (not a question -- no accent)

teachings.notes: Many native Spanish speakers make mistakes with these accents in writing.

vocabulary.derivation: Related to 'salute'; also used when toasting
